channel.title: Comments for page "Three Short Scenes About Death"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Three Short Scenes About Death"

item.title: Re: The last short scene about Death.
item.content:encoded: Sorry for replying so extremely late, but the last scene describes the All-Death, eldest of the Brothers, visiting the Scarlet King on his birthday, as he does every year, in order to put him in his place. It's from the point of view of the Prince of Many Faces, a high-ranking lieutenant of the King.

item.title: The last short scene about Death.
item.content:encoded:
What's up with that scene? Nothing. I don't understand what happened in the last short scene about death which seemed to be a little longer than the others. What I understood so far is that those characters are mythical creatures or something like that. But can someone explain to me what exactly happened in the last scene?

By the way, take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorite tales on the site, tbh. 1440's canon is one of my favorites, and you've got me hooked on it here Dmatix. Love the brothers' individual personalities, especially how amusing they seem to find us human beings(and the Scarlet King apparently).

Take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The third part was my favorite; it's nice to see the Scarlet King get taken down a few pegs. A great read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dug the second and third sections. The first felt like it didn't really support the sheer weight of the writing for whatever reason. Still, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A quick google search shows that "leech" is the correct spelling for the horrible blood worms. "Leach" is a verb referring to draining liquid from a particulate substrate.

I make the same mistake pretty much all the time. I looked it up just to be sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last story really got my think meats roiling. How it started, why it continues, how it's going to end. Take this +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the part with the youngest brother. But what cemented the story for me were the last two words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was my take as well - that the sound of his footsteps was comparable to the sound of poisonous leeches falling into the town well. Good imagery.

+1 on the story overall, particularly the motivations of each Death. Never what you might have expected, but each logical in its own milieu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Admittedly, I don't think I've ever laughed so much after reading a small clump of words as I did when Death asked "YOU DRUNK I'M THINK, DON'T YOU?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought that (about the simile) in the forum, but couldn't work out how one thing (the footsteps) could fall in the same way that another thing (the poison) leaches.

Which suggests it's not a spelling mistake, poison is an adjective, not a noun, and the footsteps fall on the streets like "poison leeches" fall into a well. I quite like the idea of someone poisoning a well by throwing leeches in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though that should indeed be "leaches", I don't think there's actually a tense disagreement. If I'm reading that sentence right, it's actually a simile — how did Death enter the town? As poison enters a well. That said, it's not immediately clear that that is a simile rather than just describing an event happening at the same time as Death enters the village, so perhaps that should be reworded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm rather more fond of "sad drunk existential crisis Death" myself, but those were nice too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i just finished Pratchett's Mort, and this has got some of that vibe, I love it.

Yessssssss Death being puzzled by canapes and dancing and fishing is the best hahahaha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spotted some errors:

the footsteps of the approaching stranger fell upon the quiet village streets as poison leeches into an unguarded well

Tense disagreement with "leeches", and it should be "leaches".

The Prince has never before seen him thus.

Same thing, unless it's deliberate.

Edit: i just finished Pratchett's Mort, and this has got some of that vibe, I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It very much was intentional, especially considering this is the Great Death's section, the one dealing with death on a massive, impersonal scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I really like about the second scene, the one with the Great Death, is that it's fairly obvious that the warrior is fighting in the Middle East, but it's not made clear whether he's an Islamic insurgent or a member of a Coalition force. You chose to leave it ambiguous while still managing to make the character both admirable and relatable, which helped to accent the attitude the Brothers have adopted towards humanity.

If this wasn't your intention and I'm just reading way too much into it, I apologize, and I like the tale regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved the second scene, probably because its closer to realism and easier to get absorbed in.

That being said, each scene is done wonderfully, and any possible points where you could have depicted something tastelessly, you don't and make it poignant and meaningful and powerful. The imagery here is good, and most of the characters I can get invested in and follow, or buy. Plus, it helps that it involves a mythos related to one of my favorite SCPs on the site (1440), which definitely helps to swallow this in that regard! I can dig this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite part of this is how between the two of us we've taken the same concept and put our own twists on them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Many thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always a pleasure reading your beautiful imagery, Dmatix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This jives very well with me. Your growing 1440 canon is one of my favorites on the site right now, Dmatix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Brothers Death return, in a tale featuring each one individually. Some aspects of these scenes might return in later tales, such as the Diagonostician and the Prince of Many Faces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Little Death is my favorite. I like the scenes.
